skill.name: dry-refactoring
skill.description: Guides systematic DRY refactoring by helping identify duplication, extract reusable abstractions, replace repeated code, and verify behavior with tests.
skill.summary: Apply a four-step DRY refactoring workflow to reduce duplicated code safely.
skill.author: YYH211

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe matches, but review found they are Markdown code fences, code examples, and ordinary refactoring guidance. No malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or executable helper script was found. A low risk remains because the documentation includes example git and npm commands that should be run only in the user's intended repository.

low_findings.title: Documented Git and Test Commands
low_findings.description: The skill includes example commands such as git checkout, npm test, git add, and git commit as part of a refactoring workflow. These are legitimate development commands, but they can modify repository state if followed without user intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly visible in Markdown examples and match the skill's refactoring purpose. They are not hidden or automatically executed, so the risk is limited to user-approved command use.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Matches Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts correspond to fenced code blocks and inline examples across SKILL.md. The package contains only documentation and no script that automatically launches shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed the flagged regions are documentation code fences and illustrative snippets. No executable wrapper, install hook, or runtime entry point was found in the analyzed file list.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is an Illustrative Placeholder
low_findings.description: The network alert points to an example API URL used to explain hardcoded strings and magic values. There is no fetch, curl, telemetry, webhook, or other network operation tied to this URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears inside a bullet about magic strings, not in executable code. Search found no network-capable instruction that sends data to this address.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Token False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are caused by ordinary words and example identifiers in refactoring snippets. No hashing, encryption, credential collection, host probing, or system inventory behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations contain words like describe, discount examples, and Map accessors, not cryptographic APIs or reconnaissance commands. The findings align with scanner token collisions rather than semantic risk.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Alert Explained by Documentation Content
low_findings.description: The high entropy alert applies to a Markdown document with bilingual prose, many code snippets, and punctuation-heavy examples. No binary blob, encoded payload, encrypted content, or obfuscated instruction block was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is a long Markdown guide, and targeted review found readable text throughout. The entropy signal is plausible for mixed-language documentation and does not indicate hidden payloads here.

content.value_statement: Repeated code increases maintenance cost and makes behavior harder to change safely. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a structured DRY refactoring workflow.
actual_capabilities: Identifies copy-paste duplication, magic numbers, repeated strings, and similar control flow.
Separates stable logic from variable parts before creating an abstraction.
Recommends functions, classes, modules, constants, or higher-order helpers based on the duplication type.
Guides replacement of duplicated code while preserving behavior.
Includes verification steps that use tests and review checklists after refactoring.
Warns about over-abstraction and unclear shared interfaces.
limitations: It does not automatically prove semantic equivalence after a refactor.
It depends on the assistant having enough project context to identify safe abstractions.
It may need project-specific test commands supplied by the user.
It is focused on DRY refactoring, not broad architecture redesign.

use_cases.title: Clean repeated service logic
use_cases.description: Extract repeated validation, pricing, or lookup logic into clear reusable helpers.

use_cases.title: Reduce frontend component duplication
use_cases.description: Find repeated UI behavior and move shared logic into components, hooks, or utilities.

use_cases.title: Review refactoring candidates
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to decide whether duplication should be abstracted or left explicit.

prompt_templates.title: Find duplication
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file for DRY violations. List repeated logic, magic values, and duplicated structures before suggesting changes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a small refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the DRY workflow to this module. Identify the repeated parts, name the stable abstraction, and propose a minimal safe change.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor with tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this repeated logic using DRY principles. Preserve behavior, update call sites, and add or adjust tests that prove equivalence.

prompt_templates.title: Assess abstraction quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this refactor for over-abstraction, unclear naming, hidden coupling, and missing edge cases. Recommend only changes that improve maintainability.

output_examples.input: A user service and product service both validate required fields with repeated conditional checks.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify the shared validation rule, propose a reusable required-field validator, and list tests for missing and valid fields.

output_examples.input: Several pricing functions repeat discount thresholds and percentage calculations.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend central discount constants, a shared calculation helper, and focused tests for each discount category.

output_examples.input: A proposed helper combines unrelated checkout, inventory, and logging behavior.
output_examples.output: The skill would flag over-abstraction and suggest smaller abstractions with clearer responsibilities.

best_practices: Refactor one repeated area at a time and verify behavior immediately.
Name abstractions after the business rule or behavior they represent.
Keep tests close to the changed behavior before replacing all call sites.
anti_patterns: Do not create a generic helper only because two blocks look similar.
Do not hide unrelated behavior behind one shared abstraction.
Do not remove duplication without checking edge cases and existing tests.

faq.question: What does this skill help refactor?
faq.answer: It helps refactor repeated logic, magic values, duplicated validation, repeated calculations, and similar control flow.

faq.question: Can it edit code directly?
faq.answer: It can guide an assistant that edits code, but the assistant still needs access to the project files and tests.

faq.question: Does it always recommend abstraction?
faq.answer: No. It includes guidance to avoid over-abstraction when duplication is accidental or clearer when left explicit.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill is documentation only and does not require network access.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Use it on small changes, keep tests running, and review every abstraction before replacing all duplicated call sites.
